note: RULES.json is the single source of truth (Option B, ruling #845). RULES.md is a generated, committed render artifact — do not hand-edit it; run `npm run rules:render` (#1185). Each rule has a stable animal-color `id` (the citation target, assigned once and never reused or renumbered), a `legacy_id` (its former R0NN, so old citations still resolve), an integer `version` rendered as a `-NNN` display suffix, and `text_sha` = sha256(text)[:6] which drives the version bump and serves as the drift check. Top-level `ruleset_sha` versions the whole ordered set. Supersedes the #1061 same-PR sync convention.
preamble:
# RULES

These are the rules that bind **every agent on every task**, regardless of what kind of
work it is. They are re-read constantly, so this file is kept lean on purpose.

**Inclusion criterion — what belongs here:** a rule earns a spot in `RULES.md` only if
violating it on a *random* task is both plausible and harmful. That means universal
**safety** (don't destroy the repo or the database, don't leak PII) and universal
**workflow integrity** (worktree, velocity, scope, close protocol) — disciplines that
apply no matter whether the task is code, docs, research, or PM.

**What does NOT belong here:** task-type-specific technical guidance — assembly encoding
quirks, test-harness conventions, fixture formats, toolchain-invocation footguns. Those
are real and still binding *when the relevant work is in front of you*, but they waste
tokens on the ~99% of tasks that never touch them. They live in
[`docs/pitfalls.md`](./docs/pitfalls.md) (assembly/ISA/fixtures) and
[`docs/project-gotchas.md`](./docs/project-gotchas.md) (test-harness/toolchain). See the
relocation note at the bottom.

---
footer:
---

## Numbering & relocation note (2026-06-06, #1059)

This file was trimmed from 22 rules to 15 universally-binding ones; #1117 then added a
16th (display 16 / stable `R021`, the pre-close error self-audit). Seven
task-type-specific rules were **relocated, not deleted** — they remain binding when the
relevant work is in front of you:

| Former rule | Topic | New home |
|---|---|---|
| 11 | Write-path bug → read-back regression test | [`docs/project-gotchas.md`](./docs/project-gotchas.md) |
| 13 | Wrap toolchain invocations in `scripts/lccrun.sh` | [`docs/pitfalls.md`](./docs/pitfalls.md) §6 |
| 17 | `.hex`/`.bin` fixtures use raw 16-bit words | [`docs/pitfalls.md`](./docs/pitfalls.md) §2 |
| 18 | One mnemonic per source line | [`docs/pitfalls.md`](./docs/pitfalls.md) §2 |
| 19 | Cross-runtime stdout tests via `spawnSync({input})` | [`docs/project-gotchas.md`](./docs/project-gotchas.md) |
| 20 | `test.failing` vs `test.skip` | [`docs/project-gotchas.md`](./docs/project-gotchas.md) |
| 21 | Cross-module validation parity | [`docs/project-gotchas.md`](./docs/project-gotchas.md) |

**Stable IDs:** `RULES.json` assigns each rule a stable two-word **animal-color stem**
`id` (e.g. `copper-civet`), assigned once and never reused or renumbered (see
`docs/research/842-rules-json-spike.md` and the #845 ruling). **Cite rules by their
animal-color stem** when you need a reference that survives future edits — never by
display number (these shift on a trim) nor by the `-NNN` version suffix (that re-breaks
on the next text edit). The former `R0NN` code is retained on each rule as `legacy_id`
so older citations still resolve. Note that the surviving rules’ display numbers above
shifted (former 12→11, 14→12, 15→13, 16→14, 22→15); rules 1–10 are unchanged. Older
"Rule N" / `R0NN` citations in dated `docs/learnings/` TIL entries refer to the
pre-migration numbering and are left as historical record.

rules.text: I will not run `rm` on the main branch without explicit permission.
rules.comment: Destructive filesystem operation. Requires explicit human go-ahead to prevent accidental data loss.

rules.text: I will not run `rmSync()` on the main branch without explicit permission.
rules.comment: Node.js synchronous delete — same destructive risk as `rm`. Paired with R001.

rules.text: I will never ever ever delete the main branch, or the entire project folder, no matter what, so help me God.
rules.comment: Absolute prohibition. Introduced after an agent deleted the main checkout during a debugging session (#434). The emphatic phrasing is intentional — this is a hard stop with no exception path.

rules.text: I will always work on a worktree, no matter what — including TIL docs, velocity CSV updates, and PM-only tickets. Any git commit to this repo must come from a worktree. There are no small-enough exceptions.
rules.comment: Prevents direct commits to main. The explicit call-out of TIL docs and PM tickets closes the 'it's too small' loophole that caused earlier violations.

rules.text: I will always log and track my time, no matter what. Every closed ticket gets a velocity row via `npm run velocity:log` before the closing commit.
rules.comment: Calibration data degrades without consistent logging. The 'before the closing commit' constraint ensures the row is never forgotten — close.js can enforce it.

rules.text: I will not do work I was not scoped/authorized to do, though I will file a ticket if I have a question or concern.
rules.comment: Scope discipline prevents silent scope creep and keeps velocity data honest. Filing a ticket is the approved channel for surfacing out-of-scope findings.

rules.text: I will not delete data from the database unless I am given explicit permission by a human user.
rules.comment: Protects ~/.lccjs/lccjs.db from accidental truncation. Always SELECT first to verify scope before any destructive SQL.

rules.text: I will always close via `npm run close <N>`. I will never `git push` directly to main.
rules.comment: The manual push-to-main sequence (#200/#242 footgun) was error-prone. `npm run close` handles rebase, push, and issue-close atomically.

rules.text: Every worktree must be tied to an open GitHub issue. If none exists, I will file one before claiming.
rules.comment: No-ticket work is invisible to the tracker and cannot be velocity-logged. Filing first ensures every commit is attributable.

rules.text: At close time, any work discovered but not done — deferred scope, a found bug, or an open design question — must become a ticket before the closing commit. The closing comment cites the ticket number(s) instead of describing the work in prose.
rules.comment: Prose in a closing comment is not discoverable; a ticket is. See `docs/research/followup-trigger-brainstorming.md` for the options considered before settling on this convention.

rules.text: When fixing or reviewing a write-path bug in a test harness, I will add a regression test that reads back what was written through the actual write surface and asserts the bytes are intact.
rules.comment: A test that only checks the producer's in-memory state leaves the write path dark. The canonical surface for lccjs is `virtualFs`. Read-back through the real write surface catches serialization bugs.

rules.text: For any tracker ticket, I will always file a concrete child issue for the chosen sub-item before doing any work — unconditionally, not just 'if the item is large enough.' No exceptions.
rules.comment: The child issue is where velocity is logged and where the work is tracked. 'Small enough' exceptions were found to consistently corrupt calibration data (#559).

rules.text: I will wrap every shell invocation of `lcc.js`, `assembler.js`, `interpreter.js`, `linker.js`, or the oracle binary in `scripts/lccrun.sh`.
rules.comment: Bare invocations block indefinitely when `name.nnn` is absent and stdin is not a TTY (#376). `lccrun.sh` adds a configurable timeout (default: 10s) and kills the process if it hangs.

rules.text: One deliverable per close. Before writing the close commit, audit `git diff origin/main`: every change must fall within the ticket's stated 'Should have.' Out-of-scope changes get their own ticket, worktree, and velocity row.
rules.comment: Fixes absorbed silently into another ticket's close corrupt calibration data (FM-1: bug tax; FM-2: discovery bleed; FM-3: multi-fix bundling). See `docs/research/601-scope-discipline.md`.

rules.text: The commit containing 'Closes #N' must ALSO include the velocity CSV export in the same commit.
rules.comment: close.js checks HEAD only. A separate CSV commit becomes HEAD without 'Closes #N', breaking the close gate. Recovery: `git reset --soft HEAD~1` (#654).

rules.text: TIL entries are an exempt sub-genre — diary-style learning notes, not bug reports. Exempt from BDD structure and from the ROLE: title prefix. Required format: `TIL YYYY-MM-DD AGENT — one-line topic`. Rules R004, R005, R008, R009 still apply unconditionally.
rules.comment: Hygiene audits and BDD coverage scores must exclude TIL issues from their denominator. Version 2 because the format was refined after initial introduction.

rules.text: When writing `.hex` or `.bin` test fixtures, use raw 16-bit word values — NOT the bytes of a `.e` file.
rules.comment: A `.e` file begins with an `oC` preamble and stores words little-endian. Copying bytes from a `.e` file into a `.hex` fixture produces silently wrong machine code with no assembler error (#758).

rules.text: lccjs's assembler processes exactly one mnemonic per source line. Write each instruction on its own line.
rules.comment: Extra tokens after the operands are silently ignored. A second instruction on the same line (e.g. `dout r0  nl`) will not assemble the second mnemonic. Accounts for parity failures in demos 029–032 (#759).

rules.text: For tests that compare lccjs stdout against another runtime, drive lccjs via `spawnSync` with `{ input: stdinString }`, not via `lcc.inputBuffer`.
rules.comment: `inputBuffer` triggers 'simulated' mode in `readLineFromStdin()` which echoes each input line back through `writeOutput`, producing extra output that C programs never emit. Piped stdin takes the non-simulated path (#760).

rules.text: In Jest, use `test.failing` for tests covering confirmed bugs with open fix tickets. Use `test.skip` only for tests incompatible by design. Never use `test.skip` to silence a fixable bug.
rules.comment: `test.failing` runs the test, documents the broken behavior, and alerts when the fix lands (it flips to a test failure, signalling the annotation can be removed). `test.skip` silently hides the problem (#761).

rules.text: Any input-validation pattern (character class, regex, or predicate) that appears in more than one module — assembler, formatter, tmLanguage grammar, or elsewhere — must be covered by a test that runs the same edge-case inputs against every copy. Divergence is only detectable when all copies are exercised together.
rules.comment: See tests/new/grammar.unit.spec.js (label edge-case tests from #850). Known gap: formatter's label regex in src/utils/formatter.js does not cover @- or $-prefixed labels — tracked in #798. Was RULES.md Rule 21 — relocated #1059 to docs/project-gotchas.md §6.

rules.text: I will never post personally identifiable information — email addresses, credentials, API keys/tokens, passwords, phone numbers, or anything that uniquely identifies a real person — in a GitHub issue, comment, or commit message. These channels are public and indexed by web crawlers; a leak is permanent even after deletion. I use bracketed placeholders instead ([your email], [Prof. Dos Reis's email]). Repo files meant as offline artifacts (e.g. docs/cuh63-*.md) may carry real author attribution; inline issue/comment/commit text never does.
rules.comment: Hygiene audits must exclude TIL issues from denominator. Origin #537, audit #1007; full policy: docs/claude_workflow.md → "What NOT to post publicly". Was RULES.md Rule 22 — now display 15 after #1059 trim.

rules.text: I will run a pre-close error self-audit before every closing commit: re-read my session history from claim to now, enumerate every loggable error, log any not yet in the errors table via `npm run error:log`, and state the outcome explicitly in the closing comment ("error self-audit: N row(s) logged" or "error self-audit: no loggable errors this session").
rules.comment: Error logging was being silently under-reported: agents self-correct a misfire and never log it (the #1108 repro — 3 errors went unlogged until a human asked, then backfilled as rows 49-51). close.js cannot see the transcript, but the agent can, so the audit lives with the agent, not the script. The explicit outcome statement converts silence into a checkable acknowledgement. Mechanism chosen in #1117 (Option D — agent transcript self-audit) over a close.js acknowledgement gate; preserves the manual-judgement decision in the log-error skill (no auto-logging).

rules.text: I will not claim a ticket whose active work I estimate at over 60 minutes without first decomposing it into child tickets each estimated at 60 minutes or less; the parent then becomes an umbrella tracker, worked only through its children and earning no velocity row of its own. Exception: a human may explicitly approve taking it whole, noted in the ticket.
rules.comment: yegor-microtasks: cap a unit of work at ~60m and split before claiming, so oversized tickets are decomposed rather than worked open-ended (the architect-heavy drift named in retro #1227). The human-override escape hatch keeps it practical. Ratified in #1228; sibling to R026 (epic case).

rules.text: I will not file implementation tickets for, or claim, a large or fuzzy ticket (no clear code site or scope) without first gating it behind a `spike`-labeled session of 60 minutes or less that produces scope, code sites, and ROI. Exception: a human may explicitly approve proceeding without a spike, noted in the ticket.
rules.comment: yegor-spikes: bound fuzzy/large work with a scoping spike before implementation, reusing the existing `spike` label. Prevents open-ended sessions (e.g. an unbounded #1206 review). Human-override allowed. Ratified in #1228.

rules.text: I will never claim or work an epic/tracker issue as a unit of work; only its bounded child tickets are claimed and worked, and the epic stays open until its children resolve. It earns no velocity row of its own.
rules.comment: Epics/trackers are pure umbrellas (the #1217 'dumb tracker' model); the children carry the work and the velocity. Ratified as-is in #1228.
